html:
I

It had lately become common chatter at Brightwood HospitalвБ†вАФbetter known for three hundred miles around Detroit as HudsonвАЩs ClinicвБ†вАФthat the chief was all but dead on his feet. The whole place buzzed with it.

All the way from the inquisitive solarium on the top floor to the garrulous kitchen in the basement, little groupsвБ†вАФconvalescents in wheeled chairs, nurses with tardy trays, lean interns on rubber soles, grizzled orderlies trailing damp mopsвБ†вАФmet to whisper and separated to disseminate the bad news. Doctor Hudson was on the verge of a collapse.

On the verge?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Indeed! One lengthening story had it that on Tuesday he had fainted during an operationвБ†вАФmighty ticklish piece of business, tooвБ†вАФwhich young Watson, assisting him, was obliged to complete alone. And the worst of it was that he was back at it again, next morning, carrying on as usual.

An idle tale like that, no matter with what solicitude of loyalty it might be discussed at Brightwood, would deal the institution a staggering wallop once it seeped through the big wrought-iron gates. And the rumour was peculiarly difficult to throttle because, unfortunately, it was true.

Obviously the hour had arrived for desperate measures.

Dr.¬†Malcolm Pyle, shaggy and beetle-browed, next to the chief in seniority, a specialist in abdominal surgery and admiringly spoken of by his colleagues as the best belly man west of the Alleghenies, growled briefly into the ear of blood-and-skin Jennings, a cynical, middle-aged bachelor, who but for his skill as a bacteriologist would have been dropped from the staff, many a time, for his rasping banter and infuriating impudences.

Jennings quickly passed the word to internal-medicine Carter, who presently met eye-ear-nose-and-throat McDermott in the hall and relayed the message.

вАЬOh, yes, IвАЩll come,вАЭ said McDermott uneasily, вАЬbut I donвАЩt relish the idea of a staff meeting without the chief. Looks like treason.вАЭ

вАЬItвАЩs for his own good,вАЭ explained Carter.

вАЬDoubtless; butвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ he has always been such a straight shooter, himself.вАЭ

вАЬYou tell Aldrich and Watson. IвАЩll see Gram and Harper. I hate it as much as you do, Mac, but we canвАЩt let the chief ruin himself.вАЭ

Seeing that tomorrow was Christmas, and this was Saturday well past the luncheon hour, by the time Pyle had tardily joined them in the superintendentвАЩs office each of the eight, having abandoned whatever manifestation of dignified omniscience constituted his bedside manner, was snappishly impatient to have done with this unpleasant business and be off.

When at length he breezed in, not very convincingly attempting the conciliatory smirk of the belated, Pyle found them glum and fidgetyвБ†вАФCarter savagely reducing to shavings what remained of a pencil, Aldrich rattling the pages of his engagement book, McDermott meticulously pecking at diminutive bits of lint on his coat sleeve, Watson ostentatiously shaking his watch at his ear, Gram drumming an exasperating tattoo on Nancy AshfordвАЩs desk, and the others pacing about like hungry panthers.

вАЬWell,вАЭ said Pyle, seating them with a sweeping gesture, вАЬyou all know what weвАЩre here for.вАЭ

вАЬAbвАСsoвАСluteвАСly,вАЭ drawled Jennings. вАЬThe old boy must be warned.вАЭ

вАЬAt once!вАЭ snapped Gram.

вАЬIвАЩll say!вАЭ muttered McDermott.

вАЬAnd you, Pyle, are the proper person to do it!вАЭ Anticipating a tempestuous rejoinder, Jennings hastened to defend himself against the impending din by noisily pounding out his pipe on the rim of Mrs.¬†AshfordвАЩs steel waste basket, a performance she watched with sour interest.

вАЬWhere do you get that вАШold boyвАЩ stuff, Jennings?вАЭ demanded Pyle, projecting a fierce, myopic glare at his pestiferous crony. вАЬHeвАЩs not much older than you are.вАЭ

Watson tilted his chair back on its hind legs, cautiously turned his red head in the direction of Carter, seated next him, and slowly closed one eye. This was going to be good.

вАЬDoctor Hudson was forty-six last May,вАЭ quietly volunteered the superintendent, without looking up.

вАЬYou ought to know,вАЭ conceded Jennings drily.

She met his rough insinuation with level, unacknowledging eyes.

вАЬMay twenty-fifth,вАЭ she added.

вАЬThanks so much. That pointвАЩs settled, then. But, all the same, he wasnвАЩt a day under a hundred and forty-six when he slumped out of his operating room, this morning, haggard and shaky.вАЭ

вАЬItвАЩs getting spread about too,вАЭ complained Carter.

вАЬTake it up with him, Doctor Pyle,вАЭ wheedled McDermott. вАЬTell him we all think he needs a vacationвБ†вАФa long one!вАЭ

Pyle snorted contemptuously and aimed a bushy eyebrow at him.

вАЬHumph! ThatвАЩs good! вАШTell him we all think,вАЩ eh? ItвАЩs a mighty careless, offhand damn that Hudson would give for what we all think! Did you everвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ He pointed a bony finger at the perspiring McDermott, вАЬвА¶¬†did you ever feel moved to offer a few comradely suggestions to Dr.¬†Wayne Hudson, relative to the better management of his personal affairs?вАЭ

McDermott rosily hadnвАЩt, and PyleвАЩs dry voice crackled again.

вАЬAs I thought! That explains how, with so little display of emotion, you can advise somebody else to do it. You see, my son,вАЭвБ†вАФhe dropped his tone of raillery and became sincereвБ†вАФвАЬweвАЩre dealing here with an odd number. Nobody quite like him in the whole worldвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ full of funny crotchets. In a psychiatric clinicвБ†вАФwhich this hospital is going to be, shortly, with the entire staff in straitjacketsвБ†вАФsome of HudsonвАЩs charming little idiosyncrasies would be brutally referred to as clean-cut psychoses!вАЭ

The silence in Mrs.¬†AshfordвАЩs office was tense. PyleвАЩs regard for the chief was known to be but little short of idolatry. What, indeed, was he preparing to say? Did he actually believe that Hudson was off the rails?

вАЬNow, donвАЩt misunderstand!вАЭ he went on quickly, sensing their amazement. вАЬHudsonвАЩs entitled to all his whimsies. So far as IвАЩm concerned, he has earned the right to his flock of phantoms. He is a genius, and whosoever loveth a genius is out of luck with his devotion except he beareth all things, endureth all things, suffereth long and is kind.вАЭ

вАЬNot like sounding brass,вАЭ interpolated Jennings piously.

вАЬApropos of brass,вАЭ growled Pyle, вАЬbutвБ†вАФno matterвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ We all know that the chief is the most important figure in the field of brain surgery on this continent. But he did not come to that distinction by accident. He has toiled like a slave in a mill. His specialty is guaranteed to make a man moody; counts himself lucky if he can hold down his mortality to fifty percent. What kind of a mentality would you haveвАЭвБ†вАФshifting his attention to Jennings, who grinned, amiablyвБ†вАФвАЬif you lost half your cases? TheyвАЩd soon have you trussed in a big tub of hot water, feeding you through the nose with a syringe!вАЭ

вАЬYou spoke of the chiefвАЩs psychoses,вАЭ interrupted McDermott, approaching the dangerous word hesitatingly. вАЬDo you mean thatвБ†вАФliterally?вАЭ

Pyle pursed his lips and nodded slowly.

вАЬYesвБ†вАФliterally! One of his notionsвБ†вАФby far the most alarming of his legion, in so far as the present dilemma is affectedвБ†вАФhas to do with his curious attitude toward fear. He mustnвАЩt be afraid of anything. He must live above fearвБ†вАФthat is his phrase. You would think, to hear his prattle, that he was a wealthy and neurotic old lady trying to graduate from Theosophy into BahaismвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬWhatвАЩs Bahaism?вАЭ inquired Jennings, with pretended naivete.

вАЬHudson believes,вАЭ continued Pyle, disdainful of the annoyance, вАЬthat if a man harbours any sort of fear, no matter how benign and apparently harmless, it percolates through all his thinking, damages his personality, makes him landlord to a ghost. For years, he has been so consistently living above fearвБ†вАФfear of slumping, fear of the natural penalties of overwork, fear of the neural drain of insomniaвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ HavenвАЩt you heard him discoursing on the delights of reading in bed to three oвАЩclock?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ fear of that little aneurism he knows heвАЩs gotвБ†вАФthat he has driven himself at full gallop with spurs on his boots and burrs under his saddle, caroling about his freedom, until heвАЩs ready to drop. But whoever cautions him will be warmly damned for his impertinence.вАЭ

Pyle had temporarily run down, and discussion became general. Carter risked suggesting that if the necessary interview with the chief required a gift for impertinence, why not deputize Jennings? Aldrich said it was no time for kidding. McDermott again nominated Pyle. Gram shouted, вАЬOf course!вАЭ They pushed back their chairs. Pyle brought both big hands down on his knees with a resounding slap, rose with a groan, and sourly promised heвАЩd have a go at it.

вАЬAttaboy!вАЭ commended Jennings paternally. вАЬWatson will do your stitches, afterwards. He has been getting some uncommonly nice cosmetic values, lately, with his scars; eh, Watty?вАЭ

The disorder incident to adjournment spared Watson the chagrin of listening to the threatened report of JenningsвАЩ eavesdropping, an hour earlier, on the dulcet cooing of a recently discharged patient, back to tender her gratitude. Emboldened by his rescue, he dispassionately told Jennings to go to hell, much to the latterвАЩs faunlike satisfaction, and the staff evaporated.

вАЬLetвАЩs go and eat,вАЭ said Pyle.

As they turned the corner in the corridor, Jennings slipped his hand under PyleвАЩs elbow and muttered, вАЬYou know damned well what ails the chief, and so do I. ItвАЩs the girl!вАЭ

вАЬJoyce, you mean?вАЭ

вАЬWho else?вАЭ Jennings buttoned his overcoat collar high about his throat and thrust his shoulder against the big front door and an eighty-mile gale. вАЬCertainly, I mean Joyce. SheвАЩs running wild, and heвАЩs worrying his heart out and his head off!вАЭ

вАЬMaybe so,вАЭ Pyle picked his footing carefully on the snowy steps. вАЬBut I donвАЩt believe itвАЩs very good cricket for us to analyze his family affairs.вАЭ

вАЬNonsense! WeвАЩre quite past the time for indulging in any knightly restraints. HudsonвАЩs in danger of shooting his reputation to bits. Incidentally, it will give the whole clinic a black eye when the news spreads. If the chief is off his feed because heвАЩs fretting about his girl, then itвАЩs high time we talked candidly about her. SheвАЩs a silly little ass, if you ask my opinion!вАЭ

вАЬWell, you wonвАЩt be asked for your opinion. And itвАЩs no good coming at it in that mood. She may be, as you say, a silly little ass; but sheвАЩs HudsonвАЩs deity!вАЭ

Jennings motioned him to climb into the coup√© and fumbled in his pockets for his keys.

вАЬShe wasnвАЩt behaving much like a deityвБ†вАФunless Bacchus, perhapsвБ†вАФthe last time I saw her.вАЭ

вАЬWhere was that?вАЭ

вАЬAt the Tuileries, about a month ago, with a party of eight or ten noisy roisterers, in the general custody of that good-for-nothing young MerrickвБ†вАФyou know, old Nick MerrickвАЩs carousing grandson. Believe me, they were well oiled.вАЭ

вАЬDid youвБ†вАФdid she recognize you?вАЭ

вАЬOh, quite so! Came fluttering over to our table to speak to me!вАЭ

вАЬHumph! She must have been pickled! I thought she was getting on, all right, at a girlsвАЩ school in WashingtonвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ DidnвАЩt know she was home.вАЭ

Jennings warmed his engine noisily, and threw in the clutch.

вАЬMaybe she was sacked.вАЭ

Pyle made some hopeless noises deep in his throat.

вАЬToo bad about old MerrickвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Salt of the earth; finest of the fine. HeвАЩs had more than his share of trouble. Did you ever know Clif?вАЭ

вАЬNo. He was dead. But IвАЩve heard of him. A bum, wasnвАЩt he?вАЭ

вАЬThat describes him; and this orphan of his seems to be headed in the same direction.вАЭ

вАЬOrphan? I thought this boyвАЩs mother was livingвБ†вАФParis or somewhere.вАЭ

вАЬOh yes, sheвАЩs living; but the boyвАЩs an orphan, for all that. Born an orphan!вАЭ Pyle briefly reviewed the Merrick saga.

вАЬPerhaps,вАЭ suggested Jennings, as they rolled into the club garage, вАЬyou might have a chat with old Merrick, if heвАЩs such a good sort, and tell him his whelp is a contaminating influence to our girl.вАЭ

вАЬPfff!вАЭ Pyle led the way to the elevator.

вАЬWell, if that proposalвАЩs no good, why donвАЩt you go manfully to the young lady herself and inform her that sheвАЩs driving her eminent parent crazy? Put it up to her as a matter of good sportsmanship.вАЭ

вАЬNo,вАЭ objected Pyle, hooking his glasses athwart his nose to inspect the menu, вАЬshe would only air her indignation to her father. And he likes people to mind their own businessвБ†вАФas youвАЩve discovered on two or three occasions. He keeps his own counsel like a clam, and doesnвАЩt thank anybody for crashing into his affairs, no matter how benevolent may be the motiveвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ It would be quite useless, anyway. Joyce canвАЩt help the way sheвАЩs made. She is a biological throwback to her maternal grandfather. You never knew him. He was just putting the finishing touches to his career as a periodical sot when I arrived in this town, fresh from school. Cummings was the best all вАЩround surgeon and the hardest all вАЩround drinker in the state of Michigan for twenty years; one of these three-days-soused and three-weeks-sober drunkards. This girl evidently carries an overplus of the old chapвАЩs chromosomes.вАЭ

вАЬYou mean she is a dipsomaniac?вАЭ

вАЬWellвБ†вАФthatвАЩs a nasty word. LetвАЩs just say sheвАЩs erratic. Ever since she was a little tot, she has been a storm centre. Sweetest thing in the world when she wants to be. And then all hell breaks loose and Hudson has to plead with the teachers to take her back. Oh, sheвАЩs given him an exciting life; no doubt of that! And lately itвАЩs booze!вАЭ

вАЬHudson knows about that part of it, of course!вАЭ

вАЬI presume so. How could he help it? She makes no secret of it. At all events, sheвАЩs no hypocrite.вАЭ

Jennings sighed.

вАЬRather unfortunate she has this one embarrassing virtue; isnвАЩt it? But, that being the case, I dare say sheвАЩll have to go to the devil at her own speed. We must persuade Hudson, however, to clear out and take a long leave of absence. He can take her along. Lay it on with a heavy hand, Pyle. Be utterly ruthless! Tell him it affects us all. That ought to fetch him. I never knew anybody quite so sensitive to the welfare of other people. Save that card for the last trick: tell him if he doesnвАЩt clear out, for a while, he will do up the rest of us!вАЭ

For the first half hour of their conference, which was held in the chiefвАЩs office the following Tuesday, Pyle stubbornly held out for a trip around the world, Joyce to accompany her father. Indeed, the idea had seemed so good that he had armed himself with a portfolio of attractive cruise literature. He had even made out an intriguing itineraryвБ†вАФHawaii, Tahiti, ukelelesвБ†вАФPyle was a confirmed landlubber with a dangerously suppressed desire to lie on his back, pleasantly jingled, under a trans-equatorial palm, listening to the soft vowels of grown-up children unspoiled by civilizationвБ†вАФthe Mediterranean countries, six months of hobnobbing with brain specialists in Germany. The latter item had been included as a particularly tempting bait. Hudson had often declared he meant to do that some day.

The chief listened preoccupiedly; tried to seem grateful; tried to seem interested; but as Pyle rumbled on with his sales-talk the big man grew restless, refilled his fountain-pen, rearranged his papers in neater piles, had much difficulty hunting a matchbox. Then he shook his head, smiling.

No, much as he appreciated PyleвАЩs friendly concern, he wasnвАЩt going around the world; not just now. Of course he had been sticking at it too steadily. Lately he had had it on his mind to build a little shack in some out of the way place, not too far off, and put in there from Friday afternoon to Tuesday morning, at least in decent weather, tramp, fish, botanize, read light novels, sleep, live the simple life. He would begin plans on such a place at once. Spring would be along soon.

вАЬAndвБ†вАФmeantime?вАЭ persisted Pyle, gnawing at the tip of his uptilted little goatee.

Hudson rose, slammed a drawer shut with a bang, swung a leg over the corner of his desk, folded his arms tightly, and faced his counsellor with a mysterious grin.

вАЬMeantime?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Pyle, I hope this wonвАЩt knock you cold. IвАЩm going down to Philadelphia, week after next, to marry my daughterвАЩs school friend, Miss Helen Brent.вАЭ

PyleвАЩs eyes and mouth comically registered such stunned amazement that the Hudson grin widened.

вАЬAnd then the three of us will be spending a couple of months in Europe. IвАЩve arranged with Leighton to come over from the university and take care of such head cases as Watson canвАЩt handle. WatsonвАЩs a good man; bright future. Oddly enough, I was on the point of asking you in to talk this over when you said you wished to see me.вАЭ

Pyle bit off the end of a fresh cigar and mumbled felicitations, not yet sufficiently recovered to pretend enthusiasm.

вАЬDoubtless you think me a fool, Pyle.вАЭ

Hudson took a turn up and down the room, giving his colleague an opportunity to deny it if he wished. Pyle puffed meditatively.

вАЬSeventeen years a widower,вАЭ mused Hudson, half to himself. He paused at the far corner to straighten a disordered shelf of books.

вАЬA man accumulates a lot of habits in seventeen years.вАЭ He returned to his desk-chair. вАЬSounds like the wedding of January and June, eh?вАЭ

Had Jennings been in PyleвАЩs place, his eyes would have twinkled as he replied, вАЬJanuary! What! You? January? Nonsense, Chief! Not a day over October, at the farthest!вАЭ

Pyle smiled wanly, and shifted his cigar to the other corner of his mouth.

вАЬI came by this valuable new friendship early last year when Miss Brent was made Junior advisor to my Joyce.вАЭ

Something of sympathetic comradeship in PyleвАЩs reviving interest, now that he was partially coming to, encouraged Hudson to toss aside what remained of his reticence and tell it all.

To begin withвБ†вАФMiss Brent was an orphan; parents reputable Virginians; most interesting French background on her motherвАЩs side; same kind of blood that the guillotine spilt in 1789вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬQuite pronouncedly Gallic, she isвБ†вАФat least in appearance.вАЭ

Jennings, had he been there, would have been audacious enough to suggest, slyly chuckling, вАЬOhвБ†вАФin that case we should amend June to July!вАЭ Then he would have watched the chiefвАЩs face intently.

But Pyle, who had no traffic with psychoanalysis, attached no significance whatever to the fact that the young ladyвАЩs probable temperament was somewhat on the chiefвАЩs mind.

вАЬAbout Thanksgiving,вАЭ Hudson was saying, вАЬMiss Brent, after a brief encounter with influenza, left the school and spent a few days at home. No sooner was she gone than Joyce slipped out, one night; attended a party, down in the city; defied some house rules as to hours; flicked all her classes next day; stormed until the shingles rattled when they rebuked her; and, in short, contrived to get herself suspended, notwithstanding that her recordвБ†вАФthanks to Miss BrentвАЩs influenceвБ†вАФhad been quite above reproach ever since she matriculated, a year ago last September.вАЭ

The story went forward rather jerkily. Hudson was not given to confiding his perplexities to anybody. Pyle discreetly remained silent.

вАЬWellвБ†вАФshe came home and plunged immediately into a series of hectic affairs; out every night; in bed most of the day; nervous, testy, unreasonable. I canвАЩt tell you, Pyle, how thoroughly it did me inвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ SheвАЩs all I have you know.

вАЬAt my witвАЩs end, I suggested that she invite Miss Brent up to visit us through the holidays. Twice before had she been our guest for a few days, and I had seen something of her on my occasional visits to Washington. Believe me when I tell you that this charming girl was no more than across our threshold last week, than Joyce was another creature, poised, gracious, lovableвБ†вАФa lady!вАЭ

He paused to take his bearings before going further; impelled to explain how the swift movement of events, that first evening at dinner, amply accounted for his decision to ask Helen to marry him; reluctant, even in the interest of plausibility and self-defence, to give words to the memory of that occasion. It had all been so natural; so unimpeachably right; so precisely as it ought to be! He had remarkedвБ†вАФperhaps a bit more ardently than he intended, for his heart was fullвБ†вАФhow happy it had made himвБ†вАФand JoyceвБ†вАФthat she had come. вАЬI donвАЩt see how we can ever let you go!вАЭ he had said; to which Joyce had added impetuously, вАЬWhy need she ever go? SheвАЩs happier here than anywhere else; arenвАЩt you, darling?вАЭ

Pyle recrossed his legs and cleared his throat to remind the chief that he was still present.

вАЬAs a matter of fact, Miss Brent is certain to be happier with us than she was at home. Since childhood, she has lived with an uncle, her fatherвАЩs elder brother, an irascible, penurious, not very successful old lawyer. There are no women in the family. And I have reason to suspect that her cousin, Montgomery Brent, is a bit of a rake, though she has idealized him out of all proportion; calls him вАШBrother Monty,вАЩ thinks him vastly misunderstood by his father and everybody elseвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ that kind of a girl, PyleвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ espouses the cause of homeless cats, underdogs, misunderstood cousins, my flighty, wilful JoyceвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and nowвБ†вАФthank GodвБ†вАФshe has promised to join forces with me! I think sheвАЩs making something of a mission of it, Pyle. I was quite willing to wait until she had finished school in June; had some serious misgivings, indeed, about that; but she dismissed the thought lightly. If I needed her, I needed her now, she saidвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I hope to God it works out!вАЭ

Pyle said he believed it would; moved to the edge of his chair; looked at his watch; asked if this was a secret.

Hudson stroked his jaw, his eyes averted.

вАЬI donвАЩt object to their knowingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ LetвАЩs consider it sufficient, for the present, that IвАЩm going to Europe with my daughter.вАЭ He mopped his broad forehead vigorously. вАЬThe rest of it they can learn in due time. Report to Aldrich and Carter and the others that IвАЩm off on a vacation.вАЭ

вАЬAny special word for Mrs.¬†Ashford, Chief?вАЭ Pyle paused with his hand on the doorknob.

Hudson thrust his hands deeply into his trouser pockets and walked to the window, staring out. вАЬIвАЩll tell her myself, Pyle,вАЭ he answered, without turning.

Doctor Hudson named his isolated retreat Flintridge. It was quite remote from the beaten trail of travel. A mere acre had been tamed to serve the cottage for which his hasty sketches, before leaving, were elaborated and executed in his absence by his loyal friend, Fred Ferguson, the best architect in town.

It was an inhospitable bit of country, thereabouts. Sheer cliffs, descending abruptly to the black water (a long flight of wooden steps led to the little boathouse and adjacent wharf) had discouraged such colonization as had long since developed the western shore, two miles distant. Deformed pines clawed the rocks, sighing of their thirst in summer, shrieking of nakedness in winter.

Almost from the first, Flintridge never knew certainly, for there was no telephone, when its master would appear for a weekend. It anticipated, made forecasts, baked ineffable angel food cakes, caught vast quantities of minnows for bait, and held itself in instant readiness to welcome the big man with the ruddy face (just a shade too ruddy, any heart diagnostician could have told him), silver-white hair, grey eyes with deep crows-feet, and expressive hands eloquent of highly developed dexterity.

When and if he came, it would be on Saturday, late afternoon. Once only had he brought Joyce and HelenвБ†вАФstrangers, passing them, presumed they were both his daughtersвБ†вАФbut that was merely temporizing with his promise to seek a retreat. And he now needed days off, if ever; for his young wifeвАЩs gregarious disposition and charming hospitality had multiplied his social obligations in the city.

How easily she had adjusted herself to his moods! How proud he was of her, not quite so much for her exotic beauty, as because of her exquisiteness of personal taste and the tact with which she met the rather exacting problems of fitting neatly and quickly into his circle of mature acquaintances. It delighted him that she chose the right word, wore the right costume, intuitively knew how to manage a dinner-party without seeming concerned as to what misadventures might have occurred in the kitchen. Yes; the affair was вАЬworking outвАЭвБ†вАФhow often he used that phrase!вБ†вАФimmeasurably better than he had dared hope.

Even the women liked her! They had accepted her on approval at first; but when it became evident that she had no intention of taking on airs because their grizzled spouses fluttered about her with the broad compliments privileged to fifty addressing twenty-five, they admitted she was a dear.

But, however pleasant it was for Hudson to note his wifeвАЩs growing popularity, certified to by the increasing volume of their social activities, his new duties contributed little to the reinvigoration of that fatigued aorta which had worried Pyle.

вАЬThe chiefвАЩs in better fettleвБ†вАФthink?вАЭ said Jennings.

вАЬTemporarily,вАЭ conceded Pyle. вАЬBut you donвАЩt mend an aneurism with late dinners, three a week. IвАЩm afraid heвАЩll crash, one of these days.вАЭ

Not infrequently some visiting colleagueвБ†вАФfor Brightwood now not only attracted patients from afar but had become a mecca for the ambitious in the field of brain surgeryвБ†вАФwould be driven out into the country to rusticate for a day or two. They seemed singularly alike, these brain-tinkers from otherwhere; moody, abstracted men, in their late forties and early fifties, most of them; seldom smiling, ungifted with small talk, not unusually inclined to be somewhat gruff. Hudson preferred to hold conferences with them at the lake, for their conversation would be tiresomely technical. And anyhow, men who trafficked daily with Death could not be expected to enliven a house party.

A devoted pair of middle-aged twins served as caretakers at Flintridge. What time Perry Ruggles, of the stiff knee, hairy throat, and Airedale disposition, was not tinkering the boat engine with greasy wrenches or trolling in and out of season for bass, he was teaching little patches of apathetic soil to take a maternal interest in iris and petunias. On Saturdays about five oвАЩclock, he would put on his other coat and limp down to the gate that admitted from the narrow ridge road; and, having opened it, would flick little stones off the driveway with his good foot.

Martha, his buxom sister, wrought ingenious quilts, concealed from the taciturn Perry the vandalisms of an impertinent, bottle-fed fawn; was silly over a pair of tame pheasants whose capacity for requiting her affection was as feeble as her need was great; scratched her plump arms gathering early berries in anticipation of some high moment when her pie would be approved with a slow wink, of which the learned guest, profoundly discoursing of surgical mysteries to his celebrated host, would be entirely unaware.

On Saturdays, about four-thirty, having again made sure she had laid out the doctorвАЩs pyjamas on the bed, and turning the vase of roses on his chiffonier a little more to the advantage of the tallest, Martha would take her stand before the window in the sun-parlour, her knuckles pressed hard against her pretty teeth, devoutly praying for a swirl of yellow dust and a flash of glittering nickel at the bend of the ridge road, visible through an open lane of dwarf spruce.

At the sound of gravel crunching under heavy tires, she would dash to the door and fling it open, always hopingвБ†вАФand hating herself for itвБ†вАФthat the doctor had come alone or, if not alone, accompanied by another man. She had been uneasy, abashed, and awkward in the presence of young Mrs.¬†Hudson, whose beauty had stirred remembrance of a certain pre-Christmas shopping excursion when she was nineвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ There had been a French doll, so beautiful it had made MarthaвАЩs little throat ache with longing. Her wistful eyes had gushed sudden tears, and she had put out a hand, tentatively.

вАЬNo, dear,вАЭ her mother had cautioned. вАЬYou may look at her, but you mustnвАЩt touch.вАЭ

On the broad mantel in the вАЬgun roomвАЭ (there had been a bit of chaffing about the вАЬgun room,вАЭ seeing there was only one gun in it among all the miscellaneous instruments of sportвБ†вАФgolf clubs, fishing tackle, and the like) an impressive row of silver cups testified that Wayne Hudson was no less expert at play than with the more important implements of surgery.

It was a frequent remark of his intimates that Hudson possessed an almost uncanny capacity for projecting the sensitiveness of his cognitive fingers to the very tips of whatever tools he chose to manipulate. There were nerves in his niblick, in his casting-rod, in his scalpel.

вАЬA lucky devil!вАЭ bystanders used to remark when he had successfully made a long putt upgrade on a sporty green.

вАЬAn uncommonly good guesser!вАЭ his confr√®res agreed occasionally, when some quite daring prognosisвБ†вАФprobably defining the exact location of a brain tumour on such cryptic evidence as the arc of an eyebrow, the twitch of a lip, the posture of a hand in repose, or the interjection of an unbecoming phrase into casual conversationвБ†вАФwas verified.

Among the trophies on the mantelвБ†вАФwhose inscriptions always amazed a visiting colleague, marvelling at his distinguished hostвАЩs diversity of proficienciesвБ†вАФthere was a tarnished triple-handled aquatic prize won by Doctor Hudson when, in the early days of his internship, he had taken a First in a mile swim.

вАЬStill swim?вАЭ

вАЬRegularly.вАЭ

вАЬEnjoy it?вАЭ

вАЬWellвБ†вАФitвАЩs good for me.вАЭ

вАЬKeeps your weight down?вАЭ

вАЬPerhaps. But, in any event, itвАЩs good for me.вАЭ

Some time in the course of his visit, the visitor would rag his athletic host upon the excess of his prudence; for the most conspicuous article of furniture in the вАЬgun roomвАЭ was an elaborate but not very decorative inhalator of the super-type used by lifesavers at busy wharves and crowded beaches, equipped with nickelled oxygen tanks and a complication of mechanical mysteries.

вАЬWhatвАЩs that thing?вАЭ

Hudson would tell him, briefly, brusquely.

вАЬWhat do you want with it?вАЭ

вАЬOh, somebody might fall in. The waterвАЩs deep out here.вАЭ

It was clear enough to the guest, if he ventured to press his queries, that Doctor Hudson did not enjoy any talk about aquatics. The guest found himself wondering why. Perry Ruggles could have explained, had he been disposed. There had once been a very anxious hour at Flintridge, down on the narrow pier. Not even Martha knew. The next time he had come out, Doctor Hudson had brought the inhalator, and had explained its use to the terrified Perry who thereafter stood in dread of the thing. It became a grim spectre that haunted his life. Some day, he suspected, he would be obliged to experiment with it. The responsibility constituted a steady, remorseless threat that tortured him and kept him awake nights. Some sharp, man-to-man candour had been handed to the surgeon, that afternoon, by his uncouth caretaker. It had been a long time since anybody had called Wayne Hudson a fool to his face. He accepted the degree with dignity.

вАЬPerhaps I am, Perry,вАЭ he replied soberly. вАЬYou probably wouldnвАЩt know. But, however that may be, the thing youвАЩre to keep in mind is that this top valve controls the oxygen; and if you have occasion to use it, donвАЩt get excited and forget.вАЭ

Not a few men of importance to the surgical world resident in widely-spaced cities, recalled having had brief and somewhat disquieting conversations about swimming, while guests at Flintridge, when, one Sunday morning in early August, they read the front-page dispatch which reported that Doctor Wayne Hudson, widely known brain surgeon of Detroit, had drowned, the late afternoon before, near his summer place on Lake Saginack.

At their breakfast table in Seattle, Doctor Herman Bliss read the shocking headlines to his wife, and when she had commented sympathetically, he added:

вАЬNot only very sad, my dear, but very strange!вАЭ

Pressed for explanation, he reviewed for her the incidents of a visit he had paid to his friend at the lake cottage, and the heavy constraint which had fallen upon their conversation when he had made some inquiries about his hostвАЩs enjoyment of the water.

вАЬDo you suppose,вАЭ conjectured Mrs.¬†Bliss, вАЬthat there could have lurked in his mind some vague mirage of the fate that waited for him?вАЭ

Her husband pursed his lips and shook his head. вАЬI donвАЩt take much stock in such theories,вАЭ he declared, almost too vehemently to be convincing.

вАЬBut you told me once that Doctor Hudson was вАШprescientвАЩ!вАЭ

вАЬOnly a form of speech, Grace. Nobody is prescient. However, Hudson was extraordinarily sentient; psychic to an uncommon degree.вАЭ

вАЬBut why did he persist in swimming,вАЭ inquired Mrs.¬†Bliss, вАЬif he was afraid of it?вАЭ

вАЬFor that very reason, unquestionably. I never knew a man so impatient of normal peopleвАЩs timidities, or more passionately eager to make himself independent of fear. Doubtless this was the one thing that gave him anxiety, and he was resolved to master it.вАЭ

вАЬButвБ†вАФby the same logic,вАЭ objected Mrs.¬†Bliss, вАЬhe might have jumped off a precipice, if he found himself afraid of that.вАЭ

вАЬNot quite the same thing! Here was something he had been able to do with ease, skill and safety. Now, for some reason, he had suddenly become afraid of it. An experience of cramp, perhapsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Might happen again. The fear filtered through his thinkingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Had prided himself on living in complete mental libertyвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Knew now that he was housing a dread! So long as he gave that phobia the hospitality of his mind, he would be, by that much, no longer his own man; so he decided to go to the mat with his antagonist. I fancy that explains.вАЭ

The newspaper account further detailed that by a singular coincidence the inhalator which Doctor Hudson owned, and kept at his cottage, was in use on the other side of the little lake at the exact moment of his own tragic need of it.

A few hundred yards off shore, near his grandfatherвАЩs estate, вАЬWindymere,вАЭ young Robert Merrick, alone in his sailboat, had been knocked unconscious by a jibbing boom and pushed into the water.

вАЬMust have been drunk,вАЭ indignantly commented Doctor Bliss. вАЬThings like that donвАЩt often happen to sober people.вАЭ

Excited bathers, informed that there was an inhalator at the Hudson cottage, had rushed a speedboat across for it, and after an hourвАЩs heroic exertion, were successful in restoring the young man to partial consciousness. It was said that he would undoubtedly recover.

вАЬUnquestionably! He would!вАЭ growled Bliss.

It was believed, said the dispatch, that had the inhalator been immediately available and promptly applied, Doctor HudsonвАЩs life might have been saved. The caretaker, Perry Ruggles, observing the evident distress of his employer, had rowed quickly to the spot, dived for him, dragged his limp body into the boat. Desperately, Ruggles had set forth with his unconscious passenger towards the Windymere beach, and had rowed until his strength failed. Small craft, attracted by his signals, hurried to him; found him huddled over the lifeless body of Doctor Hudson, weeping hysterically, while the little boat drifted in the middle of the lake.

вАЬNever saw such doglike devotion as old PerryвАЩs. I suppose he went in after him, clothes and all; bad leg too.вАЭ

Robert Merrick, the paper continued to explain, was the only son of the late Clifford Merrick and Mrs.¬†Maxine Merrick, now resident in Paris. He had only that day returned from an extended visit with his mother, having gone abroad immediately after finishing at the State University in the midyear senior class. He was further identified as the grandson of Nicholas J. Merrick, retired founder and large stockholder of the Axion Motor Corporation, with whom he made his home at Windymere.

вАЬHope this youngster will be able to realize how valuable a person he is,вАЭ said Doctor Bliss, putting down the paper, вАЬnow that he has had his life handed back to him at such a price!вАЭ

There was still another coincidence connected with this event. The village physician, suspecting that MerrickвАЩs head injury might be in need of more skilful examination than he could give it, had sent him in a swift ambulance to Brightwood Hospital. At that moment he did not know that the man who had made Brightwood famous for its brain surgery would be unable to see his young patient.

вАЬWhat do you suppose the boy said,вАЭ speculated Mrs.¬†Bliss, вАЬwhen he learned what it had cost to save his life?вАЭ

вАЬWell,вАЭ reflected the doctor glumly, вАЬfrom my own observation of the type of young cub whose father is dead, whose mother lives in Paris, whose doting grandfather is a retired millionaire, and who gets himself bumped off his boat by a boom in broad daylight, I should suppose he just scratched a match on the head of his bed and mumbled, вАШWhadda yuh know about that!вАЩвАКвАЭ
